questions.question: A heavy boss joins a thin wall in a sand casting, and the riser feeding the boss was undersized and froze early. Where does shrinkage appear, and why?
options: In the thin wall, because it freezes last
In the boss — it is the thermal center that froze last, and the closed feed path left its volume deficit as a void
At the gate, because metal enters there
In the riser only, which is discarded anyway
questions.explanation: Thick sections and junctions are thermal centers that solidify last; once the undersized riser froze off the feed path, the boss's solidification shrinkage had no liquid metal to draw on and became a void.

questions.question: In a centrifugally cast pipe, where do gas, dross, and light inclusions concentrate?
options: At the outer wall, thrown there by rotation
Uniformly through the wall
At the inner bore, because spinning drives dense clean metal outward — the bore is usually machined away
Only at the pipe ends
questions.explanation: Centrifugal force throws the densest material outward as the casting solidifies from the mold wall inward, so lighter gas, dross, and inclusions band at the inner bore, which is typically removed by machining.

questions.question: A 1-inch-thick steel casting is to be graded by comparison to reference radiographs. Which set applies?
options: ASTM E446, which covers steel castings up to 2 inches thick
ASTM E186, which starts at 2 inches
ASTM E280, for 4.5 to 12 inches
ASTM E155, the aluminum and magnesium set
questions.explanation: E446 is the steel set for sections up to 2 in.; E186 covers 2 to 4.5 in., E280 covers 4.5 to 12 in., and E155 is for light alloys.

questions.question: While grading a casting to a specification calling for severity level 2 in all graded categories, you confirm a small hot tear. The correct disposition is:
options: Accept — it is smaller than the level 2 shrinkage plate
Reject — cracks, hot tears, and inserts are ungraded categories with no acceptable severity level under typical specifications
Accept if the porosity category also passes
Downgrade it to level 3 and let engineering decide
questions.explanation: Severity plates exist only for the graded categories; cracks, hot tears, and unfused inserts have no graded plates because typical purchase specifications accept them at no level — found means rejected.

questions.question: A weld made by an automatic submerged arc process shows a defect on film. Based on the process character, the most likely pattern is:
options: A single random tungsten inclusion
A long, consistently repeating defect such as a continuous slag line or centerline crack, because automatic high-heat processes fail uniformly
Scattered hand-tool marks at random angles
Sugaring from a lost purge
questions.explanation: SAW is automatic and runs under a flux bed at high heat input; when a parameter is wrong it is wrong for the whole pass, so its defects tend to be long and repeating — slag lines or solidification cracking down the centerline.

questions.question: A star-shaped cluster of fine dark lines appears exactly where a welder broke the arc without filling the end of the puddle. This is:
options: A crater crack — a hot crack formed in the unfilled, fast-freezing crater at a stop
Cluster porosity from trapped gas
An arc strike beside the weld
A delayed hydrogen crack in the HAZ
questions.explanation: An abruptly terminated puddle freezes fast and shrinks toward its center, tearing a characteristic star-shaped crater crack at the stop — a solidification (hot) crack, not a delayed cold crack.

questions.question: On a single-V groove weld film, a tight straight dark line runs parallel to the weld but clearly offset from the centerline, angled along the original bevel direction. Best identification:
options: Incomplete penetration
Lack of fusion along the bevel (fusion face)
Root concavity
Elongated porosity
questions.explanation: The centerline belongs to IP; a straightish tight line displaced toward the weld edge and following the bevel plane is the classic lack-of-fusion image, usually fainter because the beam rarely aligns with the fusion plane.

questions.question: Which weld radiograph indications print LIGHTER than the surrounding weld image?
options: Porosity and slag, because gas scatters radiation
Cracks, because they are tight
Excess penetration (icicles) and tungsten inclusions, because both put more or denser metal in the beam path
Undercut and root concavity, because they are at surfaces
questions.explanation: Light areas mean greater absorption: icicles are extra weld metal hanging at the root, and tungsten is far denser than steel — everything in the other options removes metal and prints dark.

questions.question: On the bench you need the rules for selecting and using a wire-type IQI. Which ASTM standard do you reach for?
options: ASTM E1815 — film system classification
ASTM E747 — wire-type image quality indicators
ASTM E1025 — hole-type image quality indicators
ASTM E446 — steel casting reference radiographs
questions.explanation: E747 governs wire IQIs (E1025 covers hole-type plaques); E1815 classifies film systems and E446 is a casting grading set — knowing which document answers which question is the Level II skill.

questions.question: Two films from the same job read 1.9 in the area of interest: one was an X-ray exposure, one was Ir-192 gamma. Against typical code windows for single-film viewing, the dispositions are:
options: Both acceptable — 1.9 is within every window
Both rejected — minimum density is always 2.0
X-ray film acceptable (window about 1.8 to 4.0); gamma film rejected (window about 2.0 to 4.0) and re-shot
X-ray film rejected; gamma film acceptable
questions.explanation: Typical code density windows differ by radiation source: about 1.8 minimum for X-ray and 2.0 minimum for gamma, with 4.0 the usual ceiling — so 1.9 passes the X-ray film and fails the gamma film.
